description.summary: HAARP is a real ionospheric research facility near Gakona, Alaska, but it has become the focus of a long-running set of conspiracy theories that allege it is a secret weather-modification, mind-control, or earthquake-generation weapon.
description.full: The High-frequency Active Auroral Research Program (HAARP) is an actual ionospheric research instrument located near Gakona, Alaska. The programme was initiated in 1990 with site construction beginning in 1993; full operations of the 3.6 MW, 180-antenna Ionospheric Research Instrument (IRI) were achieved in 2007. HAARP was originally jointly funded by the U.S. Air Force, U.S. Navy, and DARPA, with the University of Alaska Fairbanks as primary academic partner. In May 2014 the Air Force announced its intent to close the facility; ownership was transferred to the University of Alaska Fairbanks in August 2015, and HAARP now operates on a campaign / pay-per-use basis. From the mid-1990s onward — particularly after Nick Begich Jr. and Jeane Manning's 1995 book Angels Don't Play This HAARP — the facility has been the central object of a sprawling conspiracy theory complex alleging that it is a covert weapon for weather control, mass mind control, earthquake and tsunami generation, ionospheric warfare, or other clandestine purposes. These claims have been promoted by figures including Jesse Ventura (Conspiracy Theory with Jesse Ventura, 2009), Hugo Chávez, and a wide variety of online commentators. They are rejected by ionospheric physicists — notably Stanford's Umran Inan — and by the U.S. National Academy of Sciences, who note that HAARP's radiated power is many orders of magnitude smaller than natural atmospheric phenomena (a single lightning flash, for instance) and cannot meaningfully alter weather, mass behaviour, or seismic activity.

etymology.origin: English acronym: 'High-frequency Active Auroral Research Program'.
etymology.meaning: A research program studying the ionosphere using high-frequency (HF) radio waves to excite the auroral plasma.
etymology.first_use: Programme name in use from c. 1990 (US Air Force / Navy / DARPA initiation)

related_phenomena.name: MKUltra
related_phenomena.description: Historical CIA mind-control program; HAARP claims often invoke MKUltra as precedent

related_phenomena.name: Tesla 'death ray'
related_phenomena.description: Earlier-20th-century directed-energy superweapon legend cited in HAARP literature

features.description: 180-antenna phased array operating between 2.7 and 10 MHz, 3.6 MW radiated power
Co-located ionosondes, magnetometers, riometers, and optical/lidar sensors
Located on a US Air Force-built site at approx. 62.39° N, 145.15° W

physical.morphology: A real terrestrial radio-physics observatory comprising a phased-array transmitter, support buildings, and diagnostic instrumentation. Conspiracy theories project additional clandestine capabilities onto this physical base.

abilities.description: Heat a localized region of the ionosphere by HF radio injection to study plasma physics
Conspiracy claim of triggering hurricanes, droughts, and floods
Conspiracy claim of seismic events triggered by HAARP
Conspiracy claim of behaviour modification via ELF radiation

habitat_preference: syndicated talk radio (Coast to Coast AM)
patterns: Spike in HAARP-blame claims after major natural disasters (e.g. 2010 Haiti earthquake, 2011 Tōhoku earthquake, 2017 Hurricane Harvey)
Repeated rebooting of the conspiracy after every operational milestone (full IRI 2007, closure 2014, transfer 2015)

range.description: Conspiracy theory primarily anglophone with substantial Latin American (Spanish), Russian, and Chinese-language uptake. Real facility is in Alaska.

hotspots.name: HAARP, Gakona, Alaska

location.description: Actual HAARP facility in Copper River Census Area, Alaska

hotspots.notes: Operational research facility; coordinates are public.

first_recorded.source: Begich, N. & Manning, J. (1995), Angels Don't Play This HAARP

peak_activity_periods.description: Foundational conspiracy literature (Begich & Manning, Jerry Smith)
Mass-media amplification through Conspiracy Theory with Jesse Ventura and major-disaster invocations (Haiti 2010, Tōhoku 2011)
Social-media-driven hurricane and wildfire blame (Harvey, Maria, Maui 2023)

origins.cultural_roots: 1990s American conspiracy culture, drawing on older 'Tesla weapon' traditions and Cold War electromagnetic-weapon anxieties.
origins.earliest_accounts: Nick Begich Jr. and Jeane Manning's Angels Don't Play This HAARP: Advances in Tesla Technology (Earthpulse Press, 1995) is the first sustained popular conspiracy treatment.
origins.folklore_connections: Heir to chemtrails, the Philadelphia Experiment, the Montauk Project, and 'Tesla death ray' lore.

timeline.event: HAARP programme initiated under joint US Air Force, Navy, and DARPA funding
timeline.significance: Birth of the actual research facility

timeline.event: Construction begins at the Gakona, Alaska site
Nick Begich Jr. and Jeane Manning publish Angels Don't Play This HAARP
timeline.significance: Foundational text of the HAARP conspiracy

timeline.event: HAARP IRI achieves full 3.6 MW operational capability
timeline.significance: Operational milestone; cited by both researchers and conspiracy theorists

timeline.event: Conspiracy Theory with Jesse Ventura airs the 'HAARP' episode on truTV
timeline.significance: Major mass-media amplification of HAARP claims

timeline.event: Venezuelan president Hugo Chávez accuses the United States of using HAARP to cause the 12 January 2010 Haiti earthquake
timeline.significance: Geopolitically prominent invocation of the conspiracy

timeline.event: US Air Force announces intent to close the HAARP facility
timeline.significance: Misread by some conspiracy theorists as concealment

timeline.event: HAARP is transferred to the University of Alaska Fairbanks
timeline.significance: Facility continues as an academic resource; conspiracies persist

history.modern_era: HAARP claims persist on social media, frequently invoked in the wake of any large natural disaster. The University of Alaska Fairbanks now runs annual public-engagement open houses, in part to counter the conspiracy narrative.

documentary.name: Angels Don't Play This HAARP
documentary.description: Begich & Manning's foundational conspiracy text. The book is a widely-circulated source of HAARP claims, but its scientific content is rejected by ionospheric physicists.
documentary.analysis: Often cited as the principal modern starting point for HAARP conspiracy theories.

documentary.name: Conspiracy Theory with Jesse Ventura — 'HAARP'
documentary.description: Episode of the truTV programme alleging HAARP weather and mind-control capabilities; central in mass-media amplification.

scientific.name: Ionospheric heating research (actual programme)
scientific.description: HAARP studies the ionosphere by injecting HF radio energy and observing plasma response. Scientific outputs include results in radio physics, plasma physics, and space weather, published in peer-reviewed journals such as Geophysical Research Letters and Radio Science.
evidence_cited: National Academies report (2014) on the future of HAARP

skeptical.name: Powerless against weather and seismicity (Inan)
skeptical.description: HAARP's radiated power is many orders of magnitude smaller than the natural energies of weather and seismic systems; no plausible mechanism exists for HF ionospheric heating to drive hurricanes or earthquakes.
evidence_cited: 3.6 MW radiated power vs. petajoules of energy in mesoscale weather systems
Energy of a single lightning flash exceeds HAARP's emission

skeptical.name: Cultural-political conspiracy theory
skeptical.description: Sociologists of conspiracy treat HAARP claims as a 1990s outgrowth of US distrust of government, exploiting genuine military/DARPA funding and the visual oddness of a remote phased-array installation.
evidence_cited: Pattern of attributing every disaster to HAARP regardless of physical plausibility

folklore.beliefs: Believers regard HAARP as a literal superweapon; sceptics regard the conspiracy as a folkloric attribution pattern attached to a real but mundane research facility.

literature: Nick Begich Jr. & Jeane Manning, Angels Don't Play This HAARP (1995)
Jerry E. Smith, HAARP: The Ultimate Weapon of the Conspiracy (1998)
film_tv: Stargate SG-1 — energy-weapon plotlines often associated with HAARP-style facilities
games: Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Chaos Theory (2005) features a HAARP-like plot device
music: Muse — Drones (2015) and earlier Origin of Symmetry-era references to HAARP-like technologies
other: Recurring topic on Coast to Coast AM

researchers.name: Umran Inan
researchers.contribution: Major academic researcher using HAARP for ionospheric studies and prominent debunker of HAARP weather-control claims

researchers.name: Nick Begich Jr.
researchers.contribution: Principal author of the modern HAARP conspiracy literature

researchers.name: Jerry E. Smith
researchers.contribution: Successor conspiracy author; HAARP: The Ultimate Weapon of the Conspiracy (1998)

organizations.name: HAARP at the University of Alaska Fairbanks
organizations.focus: Operates the actual HAARP facility for ionospheric research

research.ongoing_studies: Active scientific research using HAARP continues at the University of Alaska Fairbanks; conspiracy claims are addressed in popular-science and skeptical publications.

sources.title: Angels Don't Play This HAARP: Advances in Tesla Technology
HAARP: The Ultimate Weapon of the Conspiracy
